# The Significance of Real-Time Air Monitoring Assessments

August 29, 2023

Real-time air monitoring assessments involve the continuous and immediate measurement of air quality parameters to provide instant and up-to-date information on pollutant concentrations, meteorological conditions, and other relevant factors. These assessments are crucial for understanding the current state of air quality, identifying potential risks, and making informed decisions regarding pollution control and public health protection.

## **Key Aspects of Real-Time Air Monitoring**

**Monitoring Technologies:** Real-time air monitoring assessments utilize advanced monitoring technologies and instruments that can provide instant data on various air quality parameters. These may include particulate matter ([PM](https://www.epa.gov/pm-pollution/particulate-matter-pm-basics)) sensors, gas analyzers for pollutants like nitrogen dioxide ([NO2](https://www.epa.gov/no2-pollution/basic-information-about-no2)), ozone ([O3](https://www.epa.gov/ozone-pollution-and-your-patients-health/what-ozone)), carbon monoxide ([CO](https://www.epa.gov/indoor-air-quality-iaq/what-carbon-monoxide)), and volatile organic compounds ([VOCs](https://www.epa.gov/indoor-air-quality-iaq/what-are-volatile-organic-compounds-vocs)), meteorological sensors for temperature, humidity, wind speed and direction, as well as remote sensing tools such as air quality monitors.

**Continuous Data Collection**: Real-time monitoring systems collect data at frequent intervals, often on a minute-by-minute or hourly basis, enabling a detailed and comprehensive understanding of patterns and fluctuations. This continuous data collection allows for the detection of short-term spikes or episodes of high pollution levels that may be missed by traditional periodic monitoring.

**Immediate Feedback**: Real-time air monitoring assessments provide immediate feedback on the current air quality status. The collected data is processed and analyzed in real time, allowing for instant identification of potential pollution sources, high-concentration areas, or exceedances of regulatory standards. This timely feedback is essential for taking prompt actions to protect public health, mitigate risks, and address pollution issues.

**Early Warning Systems**: Real-time monitoring assessments can be integrated into early warning systems by alerting authorities and the public about deteriorating air quality conditions or the occurrence of pollution episodes. These systems can trigger alarms, notifications, or public announcements, allowing people to take preventive measure.

**Spatial and Temporal Mapping**: Real-time air monitoring assessments provide a spatial and temporal mapping of air quality data, helping to identify pollution hotspots, understand pollutant dispersion patterns, and evaluate the effectiveness of pollution control measures. Mapping techniques, such as geographic information systems ([GIS](https://www.usgs.gov/faqs/what-geographic-information-system-gis#:~:text=A%20Geographic%20Information%20System%20(GIS)%20is%20a%20computer%20system%20that,Where%20are%20USGS%20streamgages%20located%3F)), can be used to visualize the data and identify areas that require immediate attention or targeted interventions.

**Data Integration and Analysis**: Real-time air monitoring assessments generate large volumes of data. Integration with data management systems and advanced analytical tools allows for data interpretation, trend analysis, and modeling. This helps in identifying long-term air quality trends, assessing the effectiveness of pollution control strategies, and supporting decision-making processes related to urban planning, environmental policies, and public health interventions.

## **Applications of Real-Time Air Quality Assessment**

Real-time air monitoring assessments have significant benefits, including the ability to quickly identify pollution sources, respond to changing air quality conditions, inform the public about potential health risks, and support evidence-based decision-making for pollution control and mitigation efforts. By providing immediate and accurate information, these assessments play a vital role in protecting public health and promoting sustainable environmental practices.

![Plumes of wildfire smoke are visible above a parking lot, emphasizing the movement of wildfire smoke and its ability to affect air quality in widespread regions.](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f6bb9b58b09db262f667813/64aea8cf48f2dc9858fd3a0b_26160574883_df6470a3c3_k.jpg)

# The consequences of wildfires for air quality â and why it's important to have a real-time air monitoring network for wildfire season

â*Image courtesy of* [*Climate Visuals*](http://climatevisuals.org)â

TL;DR â Wildfires have a severe impact on air quality and, consequently, on human health. While wildfire smokeâs damage can vary depending on its composition and density across an area, research shows that it may not only have short-term damage but perhaps also a cumulative negative impact on human health. However, by recognizing the need for real-time air quality monitoring to collect data and understand pollution exposure during wildfires, we can protect public health.

### **Wildfires as a global issue**

Wildfires have grave consequences as a major issue around the globe. While wildfires can cause damage to human and environmental health in a variety of ways, the air pollution from wildfire smoke itself accounts for a significant portion of wildfiresâ lasting impacts.

According to [research from Stanford](https://news.stanford.edu/2022/09/22/wildfire-smoke-unraveling-decades-air-quality-gains/), wildfire smoke not only negatively affects air quality during wildfires but also negates decades of clean air gains because of the extreme pollution levels that people are exposed to.

We have seen wildfires grow in intensity and frequency in recent years as part of our changing climate â making it highly important that we both understand the impacts of wildfire smoke and work to mitigate its negative impacts.

### **Understanding the impact of wildfire smoke on air quality**

Wildfire smoke significantly degrades air quality around where the fire is burning and can also affect the air hundreds or thousands of miles away as the smoke travels.

[Wildfire smoke is composed of a variety of dangerous air pollutants](https://www.epa.gov/wildfire-smoke-course/why-wildfire-smoke-health-concern), including gaseous pollutants like carbon monoxide, hazardous air pollutants like polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (PAHs), and particle pollution, along with water vapor.Â

Particulate matter air pollution, especially fine particulate matter (PM2.5) is responsible for a [large component of the detrimental health impacts associated with wildfire smoke](https://www.epa.gov/wildfire-smoke-course/why-wildfire-smoke-health-concern), and research on the impacts of PM primarily serves as the [basis for our current scientific understanding of wildfire smoke exposure](https://www.epa.gov/wildfire-smoke-course/why-wildfire-smoke-health-concern). Read our [air quality measurements series blog focusing on PM](https://www.clarity.io/blog/air-quality-measurements-series-particulate-matter) here to learn more about the pollutant's impacts.

The variation in air pollution due to a wildfire depends on the fireâs severity and proximity as well as whether the fire burned in a more rural or inhabited area.

This can also help explain why the [air quality impacts of prescribed or controlled burns tend to be less severe than those of unprescribed wildfires](https://med.stanford.edu/news/all-news/2019/05/wildfire-smoke-worse-for-kids-than-smoke-from-controlled-burns.html).

Because wildfire smoke contains a mixture of different pollutants and can vary in its composition â such as according to what types of materials were primarily burned in the fire â [its health outcomes can also vary widely](https://www.epa.gov/wildfire-smoke-course/health-effects-attributed-wildfire-smoke).

One key health outcome is the way that [wildfire smoke exposure increases the risk of cardiovascular and respiratory-related effects](https://www.epa.gov/wildfire-smoke-course/why-wildfire-smoke-health-concern) in response to wildfire smoke exposure.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f6bb9b58b09db262f667813/64aea505acb74d64f471edc1_o_TrKJszlBHYt75Q2_UtUd7lxUWurP6doN6wmA3prCaNF9vjO3TH3_b8k0V1nh0JBRC1X47zDo__XPoRwKyiKIR2AMJt6vz4cal6VJ4lM3Bomc9Rl0PlwHLWxx-r4ON_YEveWd2_tywkQdNXfJrmHmw.png)

Fine particulate matter particles act as respiratory irritants and exposure to high concentrations can result in:

Some research also shows [additional negative effects due to cumulative exposure to wildfire smoke](https://www.epa.gov/wildfire-smoke-course/health-effects-attributed-wildfire-smoke), but these studies have been mostly relegated to wildland firefighters whose exposure may not represent that of the general population. Thus, more research is needed.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f6bb9b58b09db262f667813/64aea505acb74d64f471edd4_o7S29eCWM5BvSCAycrApAV1RWGUZI_fU2OZWJaB9OKZrtoo0-27hWOq3MyWXGzFpEvvMPOrPS4yaMyRWZ2kwvzX2O20LIWyfebtD7YU68lOlpYNnMeBDwWdfxoCtBQeBzLfsDXZNJVYop58qsbxLepw.png)

It is also important to note that these [negative health outcomes do not affect all individuals equally](https://www.epa.gov/wildfire-smoke-course/why-wildfire-smoke-health-concern). Research shows that wildfire smoke exposure may disproportionately impact:

Read our [health impacts deep dive blog here to learn more about the multitude of ways that air pollution impacts human health](https://www.clarity.io/blog/deep-dive-health-impacts-of-air-pollution).

### **The effects of recent wildfires on air quality in the United States and Canada**

While wildfires are especially prevalent in certain regions, such as the western United States, pollution from wildfires can impact areas all over the globe.

Notable wildfires have occurred in U.S. and Canada over the past decade. Just recently, [Canadian wildfires are still burning and have severely impacted air quality](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/map-canadian-wildfires-2023-where-are-the-fires-ontario-quebec/) across Canada, the U.S., and beyond.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f6bb9b58b09db262f667813/64aea506dcb0ea06544be534_ss9UQ3tsc_daksa-9rhYs5M2AEQyKwOVtYIBlZG1uB7AuNZOVEccwhYdra1Y3SMV_91PZodGb8iaBdC5jQFoZMxB7IBsWSOjrZaK8RqoTzdYAdVeDbPk11shsqZSUfiRZBu2ltVVGbYIw5lQYx2gQTY.png)

Other devastating fires in recent years include the 2021 Dixie Fire and the 2020 August Complex Fire in California. The Dixie Fire burns more than [463,000 acres and destroyed or threatened tens of thousands of structures](https://earth.org/worst-wildfires-in-us-history/). The [August Complex Fire burned 1,032,648 acres](https://wfca.com/articles/biggest-wildfires-in-us-history/).

### **The importance of real-time air quality monitoring for wildfire season**

The significance of having air quality monitoring in place during wildfires cannot be understated.Â

You canât manage what you canât measure â without knowing the actual air quality impacts from wildfire smoke, it is very difficult to know exactly what levels of harmful pollutants people are breathing in and how they can safely reduce their exposure.

Real-time monitoring enables both a timely response and public health protection.

It is essential to have multiple monitoring points as part of a hyperlocal, high-resolution network. Because wildfire smoke concentration can change rapidly during a fire, data must be collected frequently.

Wildfire smoke and associated air pollutant concentrations can also change due to terrain in different areas.

Having monitoring points further away from the area immediately impacted by the fire is crucial because of [wildfire smokeâs ability to travel thousands of miles](https://newsroom.ucla.edu/releases/hazardous-air-pollutants-western-states), as seen above.

### **The role of air quality data in decision-making and emergency planning**

Having a dense air quality monitoring network that collects high-resolution data is a vital part of effective decision-making during times of high air pollution such as wildfires.

[Brightline Defense](https://www.brightlinedefense.org/), an environmental justice non-profit organization based in San Francisco, deployed low-cost sensors in communities that have long experienced poor air quality, such as the South of Market (SoMa) neighborhood. These sensors captured the impacts of the devastating 2020 wildfire season on the air quality experienced in this area.

To learn more about [Brightline Defenseâs air quality monitoring network, read our customer story here](https://www.clarity.io/customer-stories/brightline-defense).

Boulder, Colorado experienced a devastating wildfire known as the Marshall Fire in 2021 that destroyed over 1,000 homes and spread over thousands of acres. After the fire, residentsâ air quality concerns grew, and [Boulder County Public Health](https://bouldercounty.gov/departments/public-health/) decided to deploy a low-cost sensor network to better capture the impacts and supply residents with real-time data.

Residents also had concerns about the impacts of air pollution from the debris removal process.Â

The air quality monitoring network provided access to real-time data and delivered alerts to residents in partnership with the city of Denverâs [Love My Air](https://clean.lovemyair.com/) program. Read more about [Boulder County Public Healthâs air quality monitoring network here](https://www.clarity.io/customer-stories/boulder-county).

Read our [blog here to learn more about different projectsâ innovative use of low-cost sensors to collect data and drive decision-making during wildfires](https://www.clarity.io/blog/exploring-the-intersection-between-wildfires-and-air-quality-in-protecting-public-health).

### **Protecting vulnerable populations through real-time air monitoring**

People are not affected equally by air quality events such as wildfires, and it is important to determine and understand the air pollution levels that may be experienced by different groups surrounding the location of a fire.

This is why local, high-resolution data is greatly important â only having data from regional air quality monitors does not truly tell us the exposure faced by different communities.

Having timely alerts should air quality reach a harmful level is one key step.Â

With timely alerts, individuals in areas with wildfire smoke can avoid doing strenuous activities or exercising outdoors, going outdoors altogether in some cases, and taking [other preventative measures like those listed here](http://www.aqmd.gov/home/air-quality/wildfire-health-info-smoke-tips) to protect their health.

A variety of platforms can be used, such as [AirNow](http://www.airnow.gov/), to disperse this information.

Tools such as the Air Quality Index (AQI) can also be very useful to easily translate the meaning of different air pollution levels and any activities that must be avoided due to the risk.

![](https://cdn.prod.website-files.com/5f6bb9b58b09db262f667813/64aea506d769aedd0834c0c2_m1wU88hf0qR98eVHiA4QsxnRz-1dURetC1wKSh0mXL-jGE2AUpo6yjz-qbFRAiQDVuaxBjfm7o5xxKeTI5E2s9Te8Iidte3YTSBSE5-ILCYhPC7HrXnzKrXdpJ8zyqjPSpfgQ1slWr6v2RHIwd438sc.png)

### **The need for action: Wildfires, air quality, and public health**

Wildfire smoke has severe impacts on human health, both immediately during the fire as well as following this initial exposure.

This is why it is crucial to build resilient air quality monitoring networks and input effective systems to help disperse air quality information to protect public health and reduce smoke exposure.

With our changing climate, wildfires are only becoming more frequent, and many now occur outside of the traditionally thought of âwildfire seasonâ, meaning it is more important than ever to learn how to protect human and environmental health.

Read our [blog on the link between wildfires and climate change â and what it means for air quality and human health in the long term â](https://www.clarity.io/blog/wildfires-are-increasing-due-to-climate-change-what-does-this-mean-for-air-quality-and-human-health-in-the-long-run) to learn more.

Interesting in measuring air quality during wildfire season to better understand exposure and protect public health? [Learn more about air quality monitoring for wildfire smoke here](https://www.clarity.io/air-quality-monitoring-for-wildfire-smoke) and how our [wildfire smoke emergency response kit](https://www.clarity.io/wildfire-smoke-and-emergency-response-air-monitoring-kit) can be used for effective emergency response. Additionally, watch our [Wildfires, Smoke and Air Quality Webinar here](https://clarity.io/landing-pages/webinar-wildfires-smoke-and-air-quality-building-an-air-monitoring-program-fit-for-fire-season?_ga=2.6228403.1082919519.1687891487-1501669537.1609875750) to learn more about building an effective and resilient air quality monitoring network for fire season.

###### Sienna Bishop

--- SOURCE 3: Real-time IoT-powered AI system for monitoring and ... ---
URL: https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321

SUMMARY:
Traditional air quality monitoring methods often lack real-time data analysis and predictive capabilities, limiting their effectiveness in addressing pollution hazards proactively. In response to these limitations, the advent of IoT-based air quality monitoring systems has emerged as a promising solution by providing real-time data with higher spatio-temporal resolution, although they come with their own challenges, such as lower accuracy and data quality concerns (Buelvas et al., 2023). Recent advancements in IoT technologies have addressed some of these challenges, significantly enhancing the effectiveness of air quality monitoring systems, providing real-time data and comprehensive analysis of various pollutants. These studies collectively demonstrate the effectiveness of IoT sensors in monitoring air quality, offering real-time data, remote accessibility, and comprehensive pollutant analysis, thereby contributing to public health and environmental protection.

FULL CONTENT:
Real-time IoT-powered AI system for monitoring and forecasting of air pollution in industrial environment - ScienceDirect

Real-time IoT-powered AI system for monitoring and forecasting of air pollution in industrial environment
=========================================================================================================

Author links open overlay panel Montaser N.A.Ramadan a, Mohammed A.H.Ali a, Shin Yee Khoo a, Mohammad Alkhedher b, Mohammad Alherbawi c

Show more

Add to Mendeley

Highlights
----------

*   •Novel IoT-based system for real-time air quality monitoring in chrome plating industry. 
*   •IoT sensors monitor pollutants like NH3, CO, NO2, CH4, CO2, SO2, O3, PM2.5, PM10. 
*   •AI models (LSTM, Random Forest, Linear Regression) predict pollution levels accurately. 
*   •System activates exhaust fans based on predicted pollution, preventing hazards. 
*   •LSTM model achieved 99 % R² and 0.33 MAE for temperature and humidity forecasting. 

Abstract
--------

Air pollution in industrial environments, particularly in the chrome plating process, poses significant health risks to workers due to high concentrations of hazardous pollutants. Exposure to substances like hexavalent chromium, volatile organic compounds (VOCs), and particulate matter can lead to severe health issues, including respiratory problems and lung cancer. Continuous monitoring and timely intervention are crucial to mitigate these risks. Traditional air quality monitoring methods often lack real-time data analysis and predictive capabilities, limiting their effectiveness in addressing pollution hazards proactively. This paper introduces a real-time air pollution monitoring and forecasting system specifically designed for the chrome plating industry. The system, supported by Internet of Things (IoT) sensors and AI approaches, detects a wide range of air pollutants, including NH 3, CO, NO 2, CH 4, CO 2, SO 2, O 3, PM2.5, and PM10, and provides real-time data on pollutant concentration levels. Data collected by the sensors are processed using LSTM, Random Forest, and Linear Regression models to predict pollution levels. The LSTM model achieved a coefficient of variation (R²) of 99 % and a mean absolute percentage error (MAE) of 0.33 for temperature and humidity forecasting. For PM2.5, the Random Forest model outperformed others, achieving an R² of 84 % and an MAE of 10.11. The system activates factory exhaust fans to circulate air when high pollution levels are predicted to occur in the next hours, allowing for proactive measures to improve air quality before issues arise. This innovative approach demonstrates significant advancements in industrial environmental monitoring, enabling dynamic responses to pollution and improving air quality in industrial settings.

1. Introduction
---------------

### 1.1. Background

Air pollution is a pressing environmental issue with profound implications for human health and ecosystems, exacerbated significantly by industrial activities. Industrial emissions release a variety of hazardous substances, including particulate matter (PM2.5 and PM10), sulfur oxides (SOx), nitrogen oxides (NOx), carbon monoxide (CO), VOCs, and heavy metals, which degrade air quality and pose severe health risks ([Kumar and Rajput, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib25)). These pollutants contribute to respiratory and cardiovascular diseases, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD), and even cancer, particularly affecting workers in industrial environments who are exposed to higher concentrations of these toxic substances ([Singh and Singh, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib46), [Hormati et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib17)). For instance, in the Upper Silesian Region of Poland, exposure to PM2.5 and PM10 was found to be several times higher than the national average, correlating with increased premature deaths and years of life lost due to air pollution ([Połednik, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib39)). Similarly, in Romania, significant concentrations of arsenic and PM2.5 were recorded, posing major health risks, especially to vulnerable populations like children ([Tanasa et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib47)). The adverse effects of air pollution extend beyond human health, impacting plant productivity, ecosystem biodiversity, and contributing to global phenomena such as climate change, ozone depletion, and acid rain ([Agrawal and Agrawal, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib1)). In mining areas, emissions from heavy motor vehicles and mining activities further exacerbate air pollution, leading to severe health issues such as lung cancer and respiratory disorders among workers and nearby residents ([Kumar and Rajput, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib25)). Effective regulations, policies, and technological interventions are crucial to mitigate these risks and protect both human health and the environment from the detrimental effects of industrial air pollution ([Singh et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib45)). Therefore, addressing industrial air pollution is imperative for ensuring sustainable development and safeguarding public health and ecosystems.

Despite the known dangers of air pollution in industrial settings, traditional air quality monitoring methods are often inadequate. These methods typically lack real-time data analysis and predictive capabilities, making it difficult to implement timely interventions. Consequently, there is a pressing need for more advanced monitoring solutions that can provide immediate feedback and predict future pollution levels to prevent hazardous conditions. This study aims to develop a comprehensive real-time air pollution monitoring and forecasting system tailored for the chrome plating industry. The primary objective is to mitigate health risks associated with high pollutant concentrations by providing accurate, real-time data and predictive insights into air quality. To address the limitations of traditional monitoring methods, we propose a system that integrates IoT sensors with advanced AI models. This system is designed to detect and measure various air pollutants, including NH 3, CO, NO 2, CH 4, CO 2, SO 2, O 3, PM2.5, and PM10, and to provide real-time data on their concentration levels. Our approach stands out by combining IoT and AI technologies to offer both real-time monitoring and predictive analytics. The system utilizes LSTM networks, Random Forest algorithms, and Linear Regression models to forecast pollution levels accurately. This predictive capability allows for proactive measures, such as activating factory exhaust fans before pollution levels become hazardous, thus ensuring a safer working environment.

### 1.2. Literature review

Air quality monitoring has evolved significantly over the years, driven by the increasing awareness of the detrimental effects of air pollution on human health and the environment. Historically, the need for air quality monitoring became evident after severe contamination events, which spurred the development of various technologies and methodologies to measure atmospheric pollutants ([Vélez-Guerrero et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib50)). Initially, monitoring efforts were rudimentary, relying on basic observational techniques and simple instruments. However, as industrialization, urbanization, and motorized transport surged, particularly in developed countries like the USA, Japan, and Germany, the complexity and sophistication of monitoring systems increased. These countries adopted automatic continuous monitoring instruments based on specific physicochemical or electrochemical properties of pollutants, although these systems were often too costly and complex for widespread use in developing nations ([Gupta and Singh, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib14)). The advent of the IoT has revolutionized air quality monitoring, enabling real-time data collection and analysis through interconnected sensors and devices. Modern systems now utilize a variety of sensors, such as MQ135, MQ6, MQ7, and MQ2, to detect harmful gases like NO 2, CO, NH 3, and benzene, and relay this data to microcontrollers and online servers for continuous monitoring and alerting ([Alekhya et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib2)). Additionally, innovative methodologies, such as the geospatial data analysis used in Cluj-Napoca, have improved the spatial and temporal resolution of monitoring networks, making them more efficient and cost-effective ([Camarasan et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib6)). In developing countries, simpler and more affordable monitoring systems based on USEPA concepts have been adapted to local conditions, ensuring broader accessibility and usability ([Gupta and Singh, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib14)). The integration of historical data and advanced modeling techniques has further enhanced the ability to estimate long-term pollutant concentrations, crucial for epidemiological studies on chronic diseases ([Michalik et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib35)).

Traditional air quality monitoring methods typically involve the use of robust, stationary monitoring stations that measure various pollutants such as CO 2, NO 2, NH 3, and benzene. These stations are highly accurate and provide reliable data, but they come with significant limitations. One major drawback is the high installation and maintenance costs, which restrict their deployment to larger cities and well-funded regions, leaving smaller cities and underdeveloped areas less covered ([Martinez et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib32)). Additionally, these traditional systems often require laboratory analysis, which results in poor precision and sensitivity in real-time monitoring ([Ganesh et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib10)). This lack of real-time data is a critical limitation, as it prevents timely responses to sudden changes in air quality, which can be detrimental to public health ([Kalaivani et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib22)). Furthermore, traditional methods lack predictive capabilities, making it difficult to forecast air quality trends and take preemptive measures. For instance, while HVAC systems can control indoor air quality, they often rely on extensive historical data to function effectively, which can be costly and may not adapt well to changing conditions ([Rescio et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib41)).

In response to these limitations, the advent of IoT-based air quality monitoring systems has emerged as a promising solution by providing real-time data with higher spatio-temporal resolution, although they come with their own challenges, such as lower accuracy and data quality concerns ([Buelvas et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib5)). These IoT systems use low-cost sensors and cloud-based platforms like ThingSpeak for data storage and visualization, enabling more accessible and widespread monitoring ([Karnati, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib24)). Despite their advantages, IoT systems still face issues related to data accuracy and the need for calibration to ensure reliable measurements ([Veerani et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib49)). Overall, while traditional air quality monitoring methods are reliable, their limitations in cost, real-time data provision, and predictive capabilities highlight the need for more advanced, cost-effective, and adaptive monitoring solutions.

Recent advancements in IoT technologies have addressed some of these challenges, significantly enhancing the effectiveness of air quality monitoring systems, providing real-time data and comprehensive analysis of various pollutants. IoT-based air quality monitoring systems utilize a range of sensors to detect harmful gases and compounds, such as CO 2, volatile organic compounds (VOCs), particulate matter, and other pollutants. For instance, the system designed by Hasbullah et al. employs sensors to measure temperature, humidity, CO 2, VOCs, and particulate matter, transmitting data to a microcontroller and notifying users via the Telegram application ([Hasbullah et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib15)). Similarly, Rahul A's study highlights the use of the Blynk app to fetch real-time pollution data from multiple locations, making it accessible on smartphones ([Naik et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib36)). Ziyodullayevna's research introduces a portable air quality detection device using MQ135 and MQ3 sensors, with data stored and visualized on the ThinkSpeak cloud-based web app, incorporating machine learning for data analysis ([Karnati, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib24)). The study by Kalaivani et al. discusses the integration of MQ2 and DHT11 sensors with the ESP32 microcontroller, utilizing the ThingSpeak platform for data logging and real-time alerts ([Kalaivani et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib22)). Ganesh et al. propose a three-phase IoT-based pollution monitoring system that tracks pollution levels using an internet server, providing real-time monitoring and long-term analysis ([Ganesh et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib10)). Veerani et al. present an advanced real-time air quality reporting system using Arduino IDE, gas sensors (MQ135), and GPS for live location tracking, with data integrated into a mobile app via IoT ([Alekhya et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib2)). These studies collectively demonstrate the effectiveness of IoT sensors in monitoring air quality, offering real-time data, remote accessibility, and comprehensive pollutant analysis, thereby contributing to public health and environmental protection.

AI and ML have become pivotal in air quality monitoring and prediction, leveraging various models to enhance accuracy and efficiency. LSTM networks and Gated Recurrent Units (GRU) are recurrent neural networks (RNNs) that have shown significant promise in predicting nitrogen dioxide (NO 2) concentrations, outperforming traditional methods despite challenges with data quality and generalization capabilities ([Cican et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib7)).

The integration of IoT devices with cloud-based AI models, such as the Bayesian-optimized hybrid time-series (BO-HyTS) model combining Seasonal Autoregressive Integrated Moving Average (SARIMA) and LSTM, has further improved the accuracy of Air Quality Index (AQI) forecasts by capturing both linear and nonlinear data characteristics ([Ansari and Alam, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib3)). Model Output Statistics (MOS) approaches using machine learning algorithms like Random Forest (RF) and Gradient Boosting have been applied to downscale regional air quality forecasts, with the global approach often outperforming local models in predicting pollutants like particulate matter (PM10 and PM2.5), ozone (O 3), and NO 2 ([Bertrand et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib4)).

The deployment of low-cost sensors in hybrid networks, calibrated through data-driven ML methods, has also enhanced spatial resolution and accuracy in air quality monitoring, addressing the limitations of traditional, expensive monitoring stations ([Vajs et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib48)). AI models such as RF, Backpropagation Neural Network (BPNN), and Genetic Algorithm (GA) have been used to explore the correlation between air quality and meteorological factors, with fusion models like RF + BP + GA providing highly accurate AQI predictions ([Liu et al., 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib28)).

In the context of health impacts, ML models including linear regression, RF, AdaBoost, and neural networks have been employed to study the relationship between air pollutant concentrations and pediatric respiratory diseases, with RF emerging as the best-performing model ([Ji et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib20)). A comprehensive review of ML models for air quality forecasting highlights the widespread use of deep learning models over the past decade, emphasizing their geographical distribution, predicted values, and evaluation metrics ([Méndez et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib33)).

Encoder-decoder architectures with attention mechanisms have been proposed for forecasting PM levels, demonstrating superior performance by focusing on the most relevant data elements and addressing challenges in missing data through adversarial networks for data augmentation ([Kalajdjieski et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib23)). The AIrSense framework exemplifies the use of AI for anomaly detection and data cleaning in low-cost sensor networks, ensuring reliable pollutant concentration measurements ([Rollo et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib42)).

Finally, the Genetic Algorithm-based Kernel Extreme Learning Machine (GA-KELM) has been enhanced to predict various pollutants and AQI, outperforming conventional models like convolutional neural networks (CNNs) and recurrent neural networks (RNNs) in terms of training speed and prediction accuracy ([Liu et al., 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib29)). These diverse applications of AI and ML models underscore their critical role in advancing air quality monitoring and prediction, providing valuable insights for environmental management and public health.

IoT and AI-based air quality monitoring systems have seen various real-world implementations, significantly impacting pollution management and worker health. For instance, a distributed sensing system using commercial sensors was deployed to monitor toxic compounds in a melting furnace environment, effectively detecting dangerous conditions for workers and enhancing safety measures ([Parri et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib37)). Another notable implementation is the hybrid sensor network combining public monitoring stations with low-cost devices for air quality monitoring, which demonstrated improved calibration accuracy and cost-effectiveness, making it feasible for large-scale deployments ([Vajs et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib48)).

Additionally, an IoT-Cloud-based model using the BO-HyTS approach was proposed to forecast the Air Quality Index (AQI) in different meteorological conditions, providing accurate predictions that can inform policy decisions and environmental management strategies ([Ansari and Alam, 2024](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib3)). In industrial settings, a complete IoT-based air quality monitoring infrastructure was developed, integrating real-time data collection and machine learning techniques to predict safety level breaches, thus protecting both workers and the environment ([García et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib11)).

Despite these successes, several challenges and limitations persist. Sensor accuracy remains a significant technical challenge, particularly for low-cost sensors that are prone to errors and degradation over time. Intelligent calibration methods, such as those using Bayesian frameworks, have been proposed to address these issues, but they still face difficulties in maintaining accuracy during extreme events and detecting calibrator drift ([Zaidan et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib52)).

Data integration is another technical hurdle, as the high-dimensional, spatio-temporal data from numerous IoT devices require sophisticated models to analyze and forecast pollution levels accurately. Deep learning models with attention mechanisms have shown promise in this area, but they necessitate extensive computational resources and robust data augmentation techniques to handle missing data ([Kalajdjieski et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib23)).

Practical challenges include the cost and scalability of deploying these systems. While low-cost sensors offer a more affordable solution, their maintenance and calibration can be logistically complex, especially when deployed in large numbers ([Vajs et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib48)). The need for a flexible and modular calibration scheme, as proposed in the MAQ-CaF methodology, is crucial to ensure reliable and scalable air quality monitoring ([Hashmy et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib16)). Additionally, the rapid development of AIoT/IIoT/IoT technologies brings about challenges related to safety, security, latency, and interoperability, which are essential for the effective implementation of these systems in various applications, including Industry 4.0 and autonomous vehicles ([Hou et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib18)). Ethical considerations, such as potential biases in AI algorithms and the safeguarding of patient privacy in health-related applications, also pose significant challenges that need to be addressed to ensure the responsible use of AI in air quality monitoring ([Satapathy et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#bib44)). Overall, while IoT and AI-based air quality monitoring systems have demonstrated substantial benefits in pollution management and worker health, overcoming the technical and practical challenges is essential for their broader adoption and effectiveness. [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0147651324009321#fig0005) represents a summary of the AI-based technologies used in air monitoring systems.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Real-time monitoring provides immediate insights into air quality, enabling the detection of pollution sources and fluctuations, thus facilitating quicker responses to environmental changes.
Access to up-to-date air quality data supports policymakers and organizations in making evidence-based decisions regarding public health strategies and environmental regulations.
Monitoring air quality in real-time is crucial for assessing health risks associated with pollution, allowing communities to take preventive actions to protect vulnerable populations.
